text: this video illustrates the treatment of a complicated type b aortic dissection in a patient that presented with refractory chest pain with the dissection extending proximally to the level of the left subclavian artery with no appropriate landing zone therefore a left carotid subclavian bypass was performed prior to proceeding with the t-var to perform a left carotid to subclavian artery bypass adequate exposure of the common carotid and subclavian arteries is achieved as described in a different video on this channel focusing on performing such bypass
Briefly under TCD monitoring, adequate control of the subclavian artery and carotid artery is obtained.
An end-side anastomosis is then performed between the Dacron graft and the subclavian artery.
The graft is then flushed, cut, and beveled appropriately.
Next, once adequate distal and proximal control of the left common carotid artery is obtained, an arteriotomy is made and extended with pot scissors.
Subsequently, an end-to-side anastomosis is performed between the graft and the left common carotid artery.
Lastly, meticulous hemostasis is achieved in the bed of the wound.
We then turned our attention to performing the thoracic endovascular repair of the type B aortic dissection.
Both common femoral arteries were accessed under ultrasound guidance with mini stick needles followed by the wire and micropuncture sheath.

Next, advancing catheters advanced into the ascending aora from the right groin axis, and an IVUS catheter is introduced up to the ascending aora and performed a pullback to ensure that the wire is in the true lumen.

Subsequently, a pigtail is introduced from the left groin axis site and placed in the ascending aiora, and a 22 French gore dry seal sheath is introduced over a lunderquist wire from the right groin axis site.
Additionally, a mesenteric angiogram is performed and showed patent mesenteric and renal vessels.
Subsequently, an appropriately sized thoracic endograft is then introduced and placed with its proximal end just distal to the takeoff of the bovine arch.
The stent graft is then deployed proximal to the left subclavian artery takeoff and hence covering the origin of the left subclavian artery which is now supplied by the bypass created earlier.

This stent graft has an active control system whereby the stent graft is partially deployed as it's showing here in the animation and its position can be adjusted proximally or distally to pull the stent graft into the appropriate position for deployment.
In addition, the proximal end of the stent graft can be adjusted to prevent bird beaking and to achieve more precise deployment.
Lastly, the bypass wound was then closed in layers with a 2-OPDS followed by 3-OPDS and a 4-O monocryl for the skin.
